title: Devilled kidneys
subtitle: Lamb kidneys in a sharp, spiced butter sauce
excerpt: Lamb kidneys cooked quickly in a hot pan with butter, mustard, Worcestershire sauce, and cayenne, served on toast. The great British breakfast and supper dish that requires only ten minutes and the willingness to cook offal.
sourceNotes: Devilled kidneys are a classic of the Victorian British breakfast and supper table. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes devilled kidneys as a standard breakfast dish. They are also documented in Charles Elmé Francatelli, The Cook's Guide (1861), and remain a fixture of traditional British cookery through the twentieth century.

content.text: Devilled kidneys are a dish for people who are not squeamish. The technique is simple: a very hot pan, butter, kidneys for a few minutes, a sauce made from the pan juices with mustard and Worcestershire sauce, and toast to catch everything. The kidneys must be fresh and properly prepared: the white core of gristle must be cut out, which is the most intimidating part but takes only a moment.

Do not overcook the kidneys. They need 2 to 3 minutes on each side at high heat; more than that and they become rubbery and develop an unpleasant ammonia quality. They should still be slightly pink in the centre, like liver. The sauce is made in seconds in the hot pan and should be sharp and pungent.
Halve {{lamb-kidneys}} kidneys lengthways and cut out the white core of fat and gristle with scissors or a small knife. Pat dry with kitchen paper. Season with salt, pepper, and cayenne.

Heat a frying pan over high heat until very hot. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and let it foam. Add the kidneys cut-side down and cook for 2 to 3 minutes without moving. Turn and cook for 2 minutes more. The kidneys should be coloured on both sides and still slightly pink inside. Do not overcook.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, and {{lemon-juice}} of lemon juice to the pan. Stir briefly to combine with the pan juices — the sauce forms in about 30 seconds. Spoon the kidneys and sauce over hot toast. Scatter with chopped parsley and serve immediately.

items.symptom: Kidneys are rubbery and have a strong ammonia smell
items.fix: Kidneys must be cooked quickly at high heat and served immediately. More than 5 to 6 minutes total cooking produces this result. Next time, pull them from the heat while still pink inside.

items.symptom: White core is still in the kidneys
items.fix: Cut each halved kidney open and snip out the pale, chewy core completely. It is unpleasant to eat and should be discarded.

items.symptom: Kidneys have released a lot of liquid
items.cause: Pan was not hot enough, causing the kidneys to steam rather than fry
items.fix: The pan must be very hot before the kidneys go in. If they release liquid, tip it off and continue cooking over high heat until the liquid evaporates and the kidneys colour.

items.cause: Too much lemon juice or Worcestershire sauce
items.fix: Add a small knob of cold butter and swirl to mellow the sauce. Taste and adjust.

content.text: Devilled kidneys were a staple of the Victorian and Edwardian breakfast sideboard, where hot dishes sat in chafing dishes alongside kedgeree, kippers, and devilled bones. The word devilled in British cooking refers to a preparation using mustard, cayenne, and Worcestershire sauce in combination: a consistently piquant trio that appears across many dishes of the period. Devilled kidneys declined when the elaborate cooked breakfast became a weekend rather than a daily institution, but they have remained in print in traditional British cookery collections. Their revival in recent years follows the renewed interest in offal cooking: they are among the most accessible and least challenging of the offal dishes, and the result is better than most people who have never tried them expect.
